_note: Sample evidence fixture captured from dry-run artifacts for 2026-05-08. Used for deterministic local/demo mode.

cards.evidence: Treasury said the refunding would raise new cash from private investors of approximately $41.7 billion. Treasury said that over the May-July 2026 quarter it plans to maintain TIPS auction sizes at current levels, including a $19 billion 10-year TIPS reopening in May, a $24 billion 5-year TIPS reopening in June, and a $21 billion new 10-year TIPS in July.
cards.macro_relevance: Supports a higher-for-longer term-premium/rates backdrop via continued coupon and TIPS supply rather than relief on duration issuance.
cards.portfolio_relevance: short long-term US duration; real rates; fiscal dominance; gold / inflation-hedge sensitivity via TIPS supply and real-rate backdrop
cards.thesis: Treasury refunding and borrowing guidance reinforced duration-supply pressure, fitting the same-session rise in U.S. 2Y and 10Y yields.
cards.title: Treasury refunding guidance reinforces duration-supply pressure

cards.evidence: TBAC minutes said current issuance sizes are adequate for the remainder of FY2026. The same minutes said the median primary dealer forecast for privately-held net marketable borrowing implies a $1.3 trillion funding shortfall in FY2027-28 based on current coupon auction sizes and bill supply.
cards.macro_relevance: Strengthens the fiscal-dominance and structural supply narrative behind higher long-end yields and reserve-asset diversification demand.
cards.portfolio_relevance: short long-term US duration; USD diversification / reserve-currency risk; gold; real rates; fiscal dominance
cards.thesis: TBAC minutes sharpened the medium-term fiscal supply story by flagging a projected funding gap if coupon sizes and bill supply stay unchanged.
cards.title: TBAC minutes highlight medium-term fiscal funding gap

cards.evidence: The Federal Reserve’s G.19 consumer credit release was on the calendar for May 7, 2026. Bloomberg reported on May 7, 2026 that total U.S. consumer credit outstanding jumped $24.9 billion in March after a revised $8.8 billion increase in February.
cards.macro_relevance: A strong credit print is consistent with resilient demand and less urgency for rapid Fed easing, which fits higher front-end and intermediate Treasury yields.
cards.portfolio_relevance: short long-term US duration; real rates; Fed / monetary policy sensitivity
cards.thesis: The March consumer-credit release was a same-day macro input, but the stronger market read appears to come from press coverage of a sizable gain rather than a dovish slowdown signal.
cards.title: Consumer credit release points to resilient demand backdrop

cards.evidence: In her May 7 speech, Isabel Schnabel said central bank independence is at risk. She said political attacks risk weakening the anchor underpinning long-term inflation expectations. She also said direct political pressure is arriving on top of structural forces that are quietly eroding the conditions under which independent monetary policy is effective.
cards.macro_relevance: Reinforces the regime-risk framing around inflation credibility, real rates, and pressure on fiat reserve frameworks, relevant to EUR/USD and precious-metals demand.
cards.portfolio_relevance: USD diversification / reserve-currency risk; gold; silver; real rates; monetary policy credibility
cards.thesis: Schnabel’s speech highlighted rising threats to central-bank independence and the risk to long-run inflation anchoring, relevant for FX, real rates, and reserve-diversification themes.
cards.title: Schnabel warns on central-bank independence and inflation anchoring

cards.evidence: In her 7 May 2026 speech, Schnabel said that when inflation surged in 2021, the key question was whether independent central banks would tighten even if higher rates worsened fiscal metrics via debt-servicing costs. She warned that if fiscal policy behaves irresponsibly, monetary policy may eventually be forced to monetise government debt, giving rise to fiscal dominance. She also said that if doubts about fiscal trajectories intensify, sovereign term premia could rise further and constrain monetary policy.
cards.macro_relevance: Signals ECB sensitivity to fiscal/monetary interaction, inflation-credibility risks, and the possibility of structurally higher term premia rather than a return to the low-rate regime.
cards.portfolio_relevance: short long-term US duration via term-premium/fiscal-dominance read-through; long metals via policy-credibility and debasement-risk framing; USD diversification via concern over sovereign-fiscal constraints across advanced economies.
cards.thesis: ECB Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel frames fiscal dominance and higher sovereign term premia as live constraints on monetary policy, reinforcing a hawkish institutional bias around inflation credibility and central-bank independence.
cards.title: ECB warns fiscal dominance and higher sovereign term premia can constrain monetary policy

cards.evidence: Agriculture.com reported on May 7 that July soybeans were down 10 cents to $11.84 3/4 per bushel. It said weekly net export sales for corn were 1.362 million metric tons for 2025/2026, within trade guesses and slightly below the four-week average. It said weekly net export sales for wheat were 78,800 metric tons for the current marketing year, down 45% from the four-week average.
cards.macro_relevance: Useful as a rejection card against an ag-supply-shock explanation for the session’s broader macro move; grain data did not show a strong fresh food-inflation catalyst in this window.
cards.portfolio_relevance: long agriculture exposure: corn; long agriculture exposure: wheat; long agriculture exposure: soybeans
cards.thesis: Fresh grain-market evidence pointed to mixed-to-soft agricultural price pressure rather than a bullish supply shock, with corn export demand solid but wheat exports weak and soybeans lower.
cards.title: Grain markets show mixed-to-soft price pressure, not a fresh inflation shock

cards.evidence: Minutes released on 7 May 2026 state that most members recognized underlying CPI inflation was expected to increase gradually and, in the second half of the projection period, be at a level generally consistent with the price stability target. Members agreed that moves to pass on wage increases to selling prices were continuing, and discussion highlighted wages and services prices as key indicators. The minutes say many members saw it as desirable to continue raising the policy interest rate and adjusting the degree of monetary accommodation, while judging future hikes based on wages, prices, and financial conditions. At the March meeting, some members judged it appropriate to keep the rate unchanged because Middle East uncertainty had heightened, but one member argued it was desirable to raise the policy interest rate to around 1.0 percent at that meeting.
cards.macro_relevance: Supports a medium-term normalization path for Japan policy rates despite near-term caution, with implications for global rates, yen sensitivity, and the erosion of ultra-easy policy settings.
cards.portfolio_relevance: short long-term US duration through global bear-steepening/rate-normalization spillover; USD diversification through potential medium-term JPY support if BoJ tightening proceeds; long metals via reduced confidence in permanently suppressed global real rates.
cards.thesis: BoJ minutes show a live internal bias toward further tightening once conditions permit, with underlying inflation seen rising toward target and several members focused on wages, services prices, and still-accommodative financial conditions.
cards.title: BoJ minutes show medium-term tightening bias as wages and underlying inflation firm

cards.evidence: The 7 May 2026 H.4.1 release shows securities held outright at 6,410,736 million dollars, down 1,624 million from the prior week. Mortgage-backed securities were 1,981,060 million dollars, down 6,981 million on the week, while U.S. Treasury securities were 4,427,329 million dollars, up 5,357 million. Reserve balances with Federal Reserve Banks rose 113,989 million dollars week over week to 3,032,588 million. The U.S. Treasury General Account fell 104,168 million dollars week over week to 877,761 million, helping release reserves into the system.
cards.macro_relevance: Near-term liquidity impulse may help explain lower rate vol and stronger risk assets, but the composition still points to ongoing MBS runoff and a balance-sheet backdrop that does not remove medium-term duration supply pressure.
cards.portfolio_relevance: credit/risk appetite through improved reserve/liquidity backdrop; short long-term US duration because liquidity relief does not negate structural Treasury/term-premium risk; USD diversification modestly relevant through dollar-liquidity mechanics.
cards.thesis: The Fed balance-sheet release shows ongoing runoff in MBS and a reserve injection driven by a sharp decline in the Treasury General Account, a liquidity mix consistent with easier near-term market conditions even as duration supply/term-premium concerns remain intact.
cards.title: Fed H.4.1 shows reserve injection from TGA decline alongside ongoing MBS runoff

cards.evidence: Release date is May 7, 2026. Securities held outright were USD 6.418 trillion on May 6, 2026. U.S. Treasury securities were USD 4.434 trillion, up USD 218.058 billion from a year earlier. Bills were USD 437.857 billion, up USD 242.439 billion year over year. Mortgage-backed securities were USD 1.981 trillion, down USD 191.885 billion year over year. Reverse repos were USD 322.686 billion, down USD 193.825 billion year over year.
cards.macro_relevance: Primary-source balance-sheet evidence that reserve-drain and asset-composition effects remain active beneath the surface, even as markets focus on fiscal supply and term premium. Helpful for interpreting why long-end yields can rise despite a policy hold and falling rate-vol expectations.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Short long-term US duration; fiscal dominance / structural inflation risk. Supports a view that long-end Treasuries remain vulnerable as official balance-sheet plumbing shifts and Treasury supply/term-premium concerns matter more than near-term policy-rate expectations.
cards.thesis: The latest H.4.1 shows the composition of the Fed's portfolio continues to rotate away from MBS and toward Treasury bills, while overall Treasury holdings have increased year over year. For a macro book focused on duration and debasement risk, the note is useful as a primary balance-sheet signal: QT is not a simple linear shrink, and front-end Treasury absorption is still happening even as long-end supply concerns dominate market pricing.
cards.title: Fed H.4.1 shows continued portfolio rotation toward bills and away from MBS

cards.evidence: ECB says higher energy prices are likely to keep inflation well above 2% in the near term under the baseline scenario. In the adverse scenario, oil peaks at USD 119/bbl and gas at EUR 87/MWh in Q2 2026. In the severe scenario, oil peaks at USD 145/bbl and gas at EUR 106/MWh in Q2 2026, and inflation is cumulatively 6.3 percentage points higher through 2028 than projected last December. Cipollone says fiscal support should be temporary, tailored, and targeted; otherwise public-finance sustainability concerns could lift long-term yields and weigh on investment. He also notes Europe could begin running out of jet fuel and kerosene reserves by the end of May 2026, implying possible real-economy restrictions if disruption persists.
cards.macro_relevance: Directly relevant to structural inflation risk, supply-shock persistence, and the interaction between energy scarcity, fiscal support, and higher term yields. Also supports a broader view that geopolitical resource shocks can re-embed inflation even without strong demand.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Short long-term US duration; monetary debasement hedge via gold/silver; structural scarcity via copper and broader resource exposure. Reinforces stagflation/energy-shock framing that is typically supportive for real assets and hostile to long duration.
cards.thesis: Cipollone argues that Europe is facing a renewed energy-supply shock that can keep inflation above target near term, with materially worse medium-term outcomes if the shock is more persistent and propagates through second-round effects. He also warns that poorly designed fiscal support can worsen debt-sustainability concerns and push long-term yields higher.
cards.title: ECB warns renewed energy shock could keep inflation above target and lift long-end yields

cards.evidence: The note says the Fed held at 3.50%-3.75% on an 8-4 vote, described as the most dissents since 1992. It cites March PCE at +0.7% month over month and 3.5% year over year headline, with core at +0.3% month over month and 3.2% year over year. It says the market is pricing the long end higher on supply and inflation reasons rather than on Fed cuts or hikes. The author's take says 30-year yields near 4.98% alongside 3.5% headline PCE indicate sticky inflation and rebuilding term premium. The note argues gold's weakness is a calendar move, not a regime shift, and expects the bid to firm into refunding and upcoming inflation data.
cards.macro_relevance: This is a concise market-structure interpretation of the flagged move set: Nasdaq strength, higher Treasury yields, and lower MOVE can coexist if investors are de-emphasizing recession risk while repricing long-end supply and inflation persistence. It is explicitly relevant to the duration-vs-hard-assets question.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Short long-term US duration; gold/silver exposure; fiscal dominance framing. Also relevant to copper through the argument that AI capex pulls forward demand for energy, chips, and grid build-out.
cards.thesis: Keyrock's note argues that the market is transitioning from a 'Fed-centric' rates regime to a supply-and-inflation regime in which long-end yields are being repriced by Treasury supply, tariff/inflation pressure, and capital-intensive AI buildout rather than by expected near-term policy moves. The author treats the recent pullback in gold as tactical rather than a regime break.
cards.title: Keyrock argues markets are shifting from a Fed-centric regime to a supply-and-inflation regime

cards.evidence: US farmers intend to plant 95.3 million acres of corn in 2026, down 3% from 2025. Soybean planted area is expected at 84.7 million acres, up 4% from 2025. All wheat planted area for 2026 is estimated at 43.8 million acres, down 3% from 2025.
cards.macro_relevance: Primary-source evidence that the grain complex enters 2026 with a tighter acreage setup for corn and wheat than the prior year. This matters when layering weather risk, fertilizer/energy costs, and geopolitics onto food-price sensitivity.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Long agriculture exposure: corn, soybeans, wheat. Especially relevant for food security, climate disruption, and structural inflation themes.
cards.thesis: USDA's Prospective Plantings report points to a 2026 US acreage mix that is less supportive for corn and wheat supply growth and more supportive for soybeans. While not within the last 48 hours, it remains a high-value primary input for interpreting food-security and grain-scarcity risk in current macro research.
cards.title: USDA prospective plantings point to tighter corn and wheat acreage setup for 2026

cards.evidence: Blockworks show notes state that the episode breaks down 'the oil market, AI-driven growth, and the inflation dynamics shaping the global economy.' The summary says the hosts debate 'whether policymakers are intentionally running the economy hot to preserve growth and geopolitical leverage.' The episode description says they discuss 'gold, housing, AI job disruption, consumer weakness, inequality, and why the next political and economic cycle could look even more unstable than markets expect.' Listed segments include 'Oil Inventories Near Stress Levels' (02:38), 'Run-It-Hot Inflation Regime' (26:25), 'Gold Returns To The Spotlight' (30:07), 'AI Hits The Labor Market' (40:47), 'Housing And Replacement Costs' (44:53), and 'Inflation Hits Everyday Life' (47:50).
cards.macro_relevance: High relevance to structural inflation, fiscal/monetary regime risk, real-asset sensitivity, and distributional stress. The explicit focus on oil tightness, a 'run-it-hot' inflation regime, and gold suggests discussion directly tied to inflation persistence, policy credibility, and debasement hedges.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Monetary debasement hedge: gold is explicitly discussed ('Gold Returns To The Spotlight'). Fiscal dominance and structural inflation risk are highlighted through discussion of inflation dynamics, policymakers running the economy hot, and instability in the next political/economic cycle. Indirect commodity sensitivity is relevant because oil tightness and stress-level inventories can reinforce broader inflation pressure relevant to real assets, though no direct thesis on agriculture or base metals was available from sourced material.
cards.thesis: The episode argues that a market rally is masking a more fragile macro regime: oil-market tightness, AI-driven capital concentration, and persistent inflation in essentials are widening inequality and raising the odds of a politically unstable, structurally inflationary environment.
cards.title: Oil And AI Are Breaking The Middle Class | Weekly Roundup

cards.evidence: Transcript excerpt states that the prior 40-year disinflation era was supported by China’s integration, post-Cold War globalization, neoliberalism, free trade, and central-bank independence, and that markets have now transitioned into a new regime with higher and stickier inflation since 2022. Dunne says the bond-equity correlation has become less reliable and more positive in this regime, undermining conventional 60/40 assumptions. He highlights a macro vulnerability from the combination of high public debt and high equity valuations: in recession, deficits widen as spending rises and tax revenue falls, producing step-ups in debt-to-GDP. He argues a large equity drawdown could materially hit household wealth in an economy that is increasingly financialized, which could intensify fiscal-dominance and financial-repression risk. He says consumers and markets are more sensitive to inflation after the 2021-2022 spike, raising the risk that inflation expectations could become de-anchored. He notes that in his research, trend following stands out during inflation surprises, and frames adaptive strategies as central to portfolio risk management rather than a small defensive sleeve.
cards.macro_relevance: High. The content directly addresses structural inflation risk, fiscal dominance, debt sustainability, bond-equity correlation regime change, inflation expectations, and portfolio adaptation under a less stable macro regime.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Short long-term US duration: weaker bond diversification and higher fiscal-dominance risk argue against relying on long-duration Treasuries as core ballast. Monetary debasement hedge / long gold-silver exposure: discussion of financial repression, de-anchoring inflation expectations, and weakened policy credibility supports precious-metals hedges, though this specific excerpt does not provide a direct gold call. USD diversification / reserve-currency risk: indirect relevance via fiscal sustainability and financial repression concerns, though no explicit reserve-currency thesis is developed in the retrieved excerpt.
cards.thesis: Dunne argues markets have shifted out of the long disinflationary regime into a structurally less stable inflation backdrop with higher fiscal-dominance risk, weaker bond diversification, and greater need for adaptive portfolio construction. He emphasizes that recession, equity drawdowns, and already-high public debt could interact to worsen debt sustainability and increase the odds of financial repression. In that setting, trend following and macro strategies are presented not just as diversifiers but as adaptive risk engines that can respond to inflation surprises and regime shifts.
cards.title: What's New in SG Indices in 2026? + The Regime-Adaptive Portfolio ft. Alan Dunne

cards.evidence: On May 8, the #GDPNow model nowcast of real GDP growth in Q2 2026 is 3.7%: https://www.atlantafed.org/research-and-data/data/gdpnow
cards.macro_relevance: Official update from Atlanta Fed aligns with calendar event; unchanged from prior at 3.7%, up from Q1, amid cooling labor but sticky inflation.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Stronger-than-expected growth supports S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 proxies; reduces near-term recession risks for equities.
cards.thesis: US Q2 GDP growth nowcast holds steady at 3.7%, signaling resilient economic expansion.
cards.title: Atlanta Fed GDPNow Q2 Nowcast Unchanged at 3.7%

cards.evidence: S&P 500, Nasdaq post sixth weekly win as chip stocks rally, jobs data boosts sentiment 📈
cards.macro_relevance: Flags key market move in S&P 500 (proxy) and Nasdaq 100 (proxy); highest closes reflect bullish sentiment post-jobs data.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Confirms momentum in S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 themes; overweight equities amid risk-on environment.
cards.thesis: US equities extend win streak to six weeks amid chip rally and positive jobs data.
cards.title: S&P 500 and Nasdaq Post Sixth Consecutive Weekly Gain

cards.evidence: central bank independence is under real and growing pressure... If the shock broadens, tightening will be necessary — and the ECB stands ready.
cards.macro_relevance: Summary of ECB Executive Board member Schnabel's speech (calendar event); cites fiscal/financial dominance and Iran war energy risks.
cards.portfolio_relevance: Hawkish ECB tone pressures EUR/USD lower; monitor for USD strength implications on USD/JPY, USD/CNH.
cards.thesis: Schnabel highlights threats to CB independence and readiness to tighten on Iran energy shock.
cards.title: ECB's Isabel Schnabel Warns of Tightening if Energy Shock Broadens
